attributes.stem:
A 48-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department of a rural hospital with acute haematemesis. He has a history of alcohol-related cirrhosis and known oesophageal varices. He does not take any regular medications. He continues to drink alcohol daily.

On examination, he appears pale and diaphoretic. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 90/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 95% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. He responds to initial fluid resuscitation with intravenous colloid. Terlipressin and prophylactic antibiotics are administered. At endoscopy under sedation, there is a large volume of fresh blood in the upper oesophagus obscuring visualisation of the varices.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with acute variceal bleeding that cannot be controlled endoscopically due to poor visualisation. After initial resuscitation and medical therapy, definitive bleeding control is required. Balloon tamponade using a Sengstaken-Blakemore tube is the most appropriate next step as it can achieve temporary haemostasis when endoscopic therapy is not possible. The tube has two balloons - an oesophageal balloon that directly compresses bleeding varices, and a gastric balloon that provides counter-traction (Image). This can achieve haemostasis in up to 80% of cases, though rebleeding is common when the balloons are deflated.

Repeat endoscopy after further resuscitation would delay definitive management without addressing the fundamental problem of poor visualisation. Even with optimal resuscitation, ongoing active bleeding will continue to obscure the view. This approach risks further deterioration while waiting for a theoretical improvement in conditions.

Endotracheal intubation and repeat endoscopy, while sometimes necessary to protect the airway during endoscopy, does not address the primary issue of poor visualisation due to active bleeding. While airway protection is important, particularly if balloon tamponade is planned, intubation alone will not improve the ability to identify and treat the bleeding source.

Emergency transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) is an effective treatment for refractory variceal bleeding by reducing portal pressure, but requires specialist interventional radiology facilities and expertise not available in a rural setting. Furthermore, TIPS should only be considered after failure of endoscopic therapy in a stabilised patient.

Urgent transfer to tertiary centre without first achieving haemostasis would be dangerous. The patient requires stabilisation and bleeding control before transfer can be safely undertaken. Transfer should only occur after temporary control has been achieved with balloon tamponade.

After balloon tamponade is achieved, the patient should be transferred to a tertiary centre for definitive management. The tamponade balloon should not remain inflated for more than 24 hours due to risk of pressure necrosis. During this time, the patient should be closely monitored for complications including aspiration, oesophageal rupture, and airway compromise.
learningPoints: Balloon tamponade with a Sengstaken-Blakemore tube is indicated when endoscopic visualisation of bleeding varices is not possible due to blood obscuring the view.
Initial management of variceal bleeding includes fluid resuscitation, terlipressin, and prophylactic antibiotics.
Endoscopic therapy (banding/sclerotherapy) is first-line treatment for variceal bleeding when varices can be visualised.
references: Garcia-Tsao G, Bosch J. Management of varices and variceal hemorrhage in cirrhosis. N Engl J Med 2010;362(9):823–832
Bauer J, Kreel I, Kark A. The Use of the Sengstaken-Blakemore Tube for Immediate Control of Bleeding Esophageal Varices. Annals of Surgery, 1974;179(3):273–277.

Hunt P, Korman M, G., Hansky, J., & Parkin, W. G. (1982). An 8-year prospective experience with balloon tamponade in emergency control of bleeding esophageal varices. Digestive Diseases and Sciences, 27(5), 413–416
Garcia-Pagan JC, Caca K, Bureau C, Laleman W, Appenrodt B, Luca A, et al. Early use of TIPS in patients with cirrhosis and variceal bleeding. N Engl J Med 2010;362(25):2370–9

Lv Y, Yang Z, Liu L, et al. Early TIPS with covered stents versus standard treatment for acute variceal bleeding in patients with advanced cirrhosis: a randomised controlled trial. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2019; 4(8):587-598.
